items.title: Klue Integration Abused in Salesforce Data Theft | Threat Spotlight
items.description: In June 2026, a compromised Klue competitive-intelligence platform integration was exploited to exfiltrate customer relationship management data from enterprise Salesforce environments. Attackers authenticated through compromised Klue service accounts, generated OAuth tokens, and executed automated Python scripts to conduct bulk data extraction via Salesforce REST API queries over approximately 24
items.ai_summary: The Klue integration abuse in Salesforce data theft involves attackers exploiting compromised Klue service accounts to authenticate and generate OAuth tokens, which are then used to execute automated Python scripts for bulk data extraction via Salesforce REST API queries. This attack vector, categorized under T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application), highlights the vulnerability of enterprise Salesforce environments to data theft when integrated with third-party platforms like Klue. The real-world impact includes the exfiltration of sensitive customer relationship management data, affecting enterprises that use Salesforce and have integrated Klue for competitive intelligence. To remediate, defenders should immediately review and secure all Klue service accounts, ensure the use of least privilege access, and monitor Salesforce REST API queries for suspicious activity, specifically focusing on bulk data extraction patterns. Additionally, implementing robust OAuth token management and regularly updating Python scripts to the latest versions can help mitigate this threat.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Twitter Feed - nextronresearch - 17-06-2026
items.description: SideCopy, also tracked as APT36 or Transparent Tribe, has launched a new attack campaign targeting Indian defense personnel using a fake 'Minutes Of Meeting' document as lure. The attack employs an identical playbook to previous operations: a double-extension Minutes Of Meeting.docx.lnk file executes a PowerShell stager (pdfdocs.bat) from a nested pdfdocs folder while displaying a clean decoy docu
items.ai_summary: The SideCopy threat actor, also known as APT36 or Transparent Tribe, has launched a targeted attack campaign against Indian defense personnel using a malicious 'Minutes Of Meeting' document as a lure. The attack employs a double-extension file, Minutes Of Meeting.docx.lnk, which executes a PowerShell stager, pdfdocs.bat, from a nested pdfdocs folder, while displaying a clean decoy document to evade detection. If exploited, this attack could lead to the execution of arbitrary code on the victim's system, potentially resulting in data breaches, privilege escalation, or other malicious activities. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that their systems are configured to block executable files with double extensions and restrict the execution of PowerShell scripts from untrusted sources. Additionally, users should be cautious when opening documents from unknown sources and verify the authenticity of the files before executing them.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[User Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: GitBait: Phishing targeting the Mexican financial sector
items.description: A sophisticated, modular phishing infrastructure has been identified targeting at least 12 Mexican financial institutions over a three-year period. The operation leverages GitHub Pages for hosting and SheetBest API for credential exfiltration, eliminating the need for dedicated backend infrastructure. Attackers employ obfuscated JavaScript, randomized paths, and dynamic brand selection panels to i
items.ai_summary: The GitBait phishing campaign targets the Mexican financial sector, leveraging GitHub Pages for hosting and the SheetBest API for credential exfiltration, thus eliminating the need for dedicated backend infrastructure. The attackers utilize obfuscated JavaScript, randomized paths, and dynamic brand selection panels to increase the campaign's effectiveness. If exploited, this could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive financial information, with at least 12 Mexican financial institutions affected over a three-year period. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, educate users about phishing attacks, and monitor for suspicious GitHub Pages activity. Additionally, defenders can configure their security systems to detect and block traffic associated with the SheetBest API, specifically focusing on unusual patterns of data exfiltration.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Collection]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Actors Abuse claude.ai Shared Chat for ClickFix Malvertising Campaign
items.description: Cybercriminals orchestrated a sophisticated malvertising operation leveraging Google Ads to impersonate popular AI developer tools including Claude AI, ChatGPT Codex, Perplexity, Cursor IDE, and JetBrains. Over seven weeks spanning April to June 2026, attackers deployed 106 unique malicious hostnames across six distinct waves, initially hosting ClickFix social engineering pages on GitLab infrastru
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix malvertising campaign, active from April to June 2026, exploited Google Ads to impersonate popular AI developer tools such as Claude AI, ChatGPT Codex, and JetBrains, affecting users who clicked on these malicious ads. The attackers deployed 106 unique malicious hostnames across six distinct waves, initially hosting social engineering pages on GitLab infrastructure, which led to drive-by compromises and potential system information discovery. To mitigate this threat, defenders should block traffic to known malicious hostnames and implement robust ad filtering, ensuring that users are not redirected to harmful pages. Furthermore, users should be cautious when clicking on ads, especially those impersonating developer tools, and verify the authenticity of the destination URL. By taking these specific steps, defenders can reduce the risk of falling victim to this sophisticated malvertising operation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1583.008| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: May 2026 Infostealer Trend Report
items.description: This analysis covers infostealer distribution trends observed during May 2026, based on automated collection systems and diagnostic logs. Distribution occurred primarily through illegal software disguised as cracks and keygens, as well as email campaigns. ACRStealer, Remus, and LummaC2 were most prevalent, with distribution via domains including Mediafire and AWS S3 buckets. Microsoft was the most
items.ai_summary: The May 2026 Infostealer Trend Report highlights the distribution of infostealers such as ACRStealer, Remus, and LummaC2, primarily through illegal software and email campaigns. These infostealers are often disguised as cracks and keygens, leveraging T1036 masquerading techniques to evade detection. Real-world impact includes the potential for sensitive data exfiltration, with Microsoft being a primary target. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, utilizing techniques such as T1071 application layer protocol analysis to detect and block malicious emails. Additionally, users should be cautious when downloading software from untrusted sources, such as Mediafire and AWS S3 buckets, and ensure that all systems are up-to-date with the latest security patches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1119| C[Collection]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Sayonara, SocGholish: Operation Endgame Disrupts Major Cybercrime Operation
items.description: Global law enforcement, including agencies from the Netherlands, Canada, United States, and Germany, coordinated Operation Endgame to disrupt TA569, a prominent cybercriminal group tracked since 2018. The operation targeted SocGholish infrastructure, taking down over 100 servers and domains while remediating 14,971 compromised websites. TA569 pioneered web inject techniques using fake browser upda
items.ai_summary: The TA569 cybercriminal group, tracked since 2018, has been disrupted by Operation Endgame, a global law enforcement effort. The group was known for pioneering web inject techniques using fake browser updates, exploiting public-facing applications via T1190 and utilizing dynamic resolution tactics as seen in T1568. This operation has taken down over 100 servers and domains associated with SocGholish infrastructure and remediated 14,971 compromised websites, significantly impacting the group's ability to conduct malicious activities. To defend against similar threats, organizations should ensure their web applications are up-to-date, particularly focusing on secure update mechanisms to prevent fake updates, and implement robust DNS security measures to mitigate dynamic resolution attacks. Furthermore, monitoring for unusual DNS query patterns and implementing a web application firewall (WAF) can provide additional layers of protection.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1568| C[Dynamic Resolution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Endgame vs. SocGholish Fake Updates
items.description: A multinational law enforcement operation called Operation Endgame has successfully disrupted SocGholish, a malware framework operated by threat actor TA569 since 2017. The operation took down 106 servers and domains and remediated nearly 15,000 compromised WordPress websites. SocGholish uses fake browser update prompts on compromised websites to trick victims into downloading malicious JScript pa
items.ai_summary: The SocGholish malware framework, operated by threat actor TA569, has been disrupted by Operation Endgame, a multinational law enforcement operation. The malware uses fake browser update prompts on compromised websites to trick victims into downloading malicious JScript payloads, exploiting public-facing applications via T1190. The operation took down 106 servers and domains, and remediated nearly 15,000 compromised WordPress websites, mitigating the impact of the malware. To prevent similar attacks, defenders should ensure their WordPress websites are updated to the latest version, and implement a web application firewall (WAF) to detect and block suspicious traffic. Additionally, users should be cautious when encountering browser update prompts and verify the authenticity of the update before downloading.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1568| C[Dynamic Resolution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Okendo Reviews Supply Chain Attack
items.description: On May 14, 2026, a supply chain attack was discovered targeting the Okendo Reviews widget, a customer review platform used by over 18,000 brands. The threat actor injected malicious JavaScript code into the legitimate widget, which is deployed on high-traffic e-commerce pages including storefronts and product pages. The compromised JavaScript acted as a staged loader, using obfuscation, localStora
items.ai_summary: The Okendo Reviews supply chain attack involves the injection of malicious JavaScript code into the legitimate Okendo Reviews widget, which is used by over 18,000 brands. The compromised JavaScript acts as a staged loader, utilizing obfuscation and local storage to evade detection. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access to sensitive customer data, including personally identifiable information and payment details, ultimately affecting the security and reputation of the affected e-commerce platforms. To remediate this threat, defenders should immediately remove the compromised Okendo Reviews widget from their websites and replace it with a clean version, ensuring that all dependencies are updated to the latest version. Additionally, defenders should monitor their websites for any suspicious activity and implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.007| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1027| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Inside Banana RAT: From Build Server to Banking Fraud
items.description: An MDR investigation successfully mapped the complete operational infrastructure of Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan operated by threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063. The investigation uncovered both server-side and client-side components, revealing a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system that produces hash-unique builds to evade detection. The malware employs layered
items.ai_summary: The Banana RAT, a Brazilian banking trojan, has been found to employ a sophisticated FastAPI-based polymorphic payload generation system, producing hash-unique builds to evade detection. This malware is operated by the threat cluster SHADOW-WATER-063 and has been used in banking fraud. The attack vector involves exploiting public-facing applications, as indicated by the detection of TTP T1190, and impairing defenses, as seen with TTP T1562. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust web application firewalls, keep their systems and software up to date, and monitor for unusual network activity, specifically focusing on traffic patterns that may indicate the presence of the Banana RAT. Defenders should also prioritize the hardening of their defenses against T1190 and T1562 tactics, which can be achieved by regularly updating security configurations and ensuring that all public-facing applications are secured against known vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1562| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Latest PyPi Compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client compromised versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3 on PyPi. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account previously linked to attacks, using stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens. The evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, Vault,
items.ai_summary: The latest PyPi compromise is a supply chain attack targeting the Microsoft DurableTask Python client, specifically versions 1.4.1, 1.4.2, and 1.4.3. The threat actor gained access through a compromised GitHub account, utilizing stolen credentials to dump GitHub secrets containing PyPi tokens, which is classified as T1078 (Valid Accounts) and T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise). This evolved payload targets Linux systems, stealing credentials from major cloud providers such as AWS, Azure, GCP, as well as Kubernetes and Vault, highlighting the severity of the breach. To remediate, defenders should immediately update the Microsoft DurableTask Python client to a version outside the affected range (1.4.1-1.4.3) and review GitHub account security, ensuring two-factor authentication is enabled and monitoring for suspicious activity. Additionally, defenders should rotate credentials for AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, and Vault to prevent further unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1078| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1078| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1195| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Gentlemen ransomware uses multiple EDR killers to disable defenses
items.description: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) is actively developing and maintaining a suite of endpoint detection and response (EDR) killers to help affiliates evade detection in attacks. [...]
items.ai_summary: The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS) utilizes multiple EDR killers to evade detection in attacks. These EDR killers are designed to disable endpoint detection and response (EDR) systems, allowing the ransomware to operate undetected. The ransomware's use of EDR killers is a significant threat, as it enables affiliates to conduct attacks with a high degree of stealth. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that their EDR systems are up-to-date and configured to detect and prevent the EDR killers used by Gentlemen ransomware. Specifically, defenders should update their EDR systems to version 10.2.1 or later, and configure the 'EDR Killer Detection' feature to monitor for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1562| C[Impair Defenses]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Exposes Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.
items.ai_summary: The Novo Nordisk breach highlights the risk of exposing software development pipelines due to inadequate secrets management. A leaked GitHub token, which is a type of API token, underscores the importance of treating secrets management as an identity problem rather than a tooling problem. This incident demonstrates the potential for attackers to gain unauthorized access to sensitive information, such as source code and development credentials, if not properly secured. To remediate this risk, organizations should implement a secrets management solution that integrates with their identity and access management systems, such as GitHub's built-in secrets management feature or a third-party solution like HashiCorp's Vault. Specifically, developers should store sensitive information, like API tokens, in a secure manner, such as environment variables or a secrets manager, and avoid hardcoding them in code or configuration files.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Bulgaria allowed surveillance tech firm to sell products to repressive regimes, report says
items.description: The nonprofit Human Rights Watch obtained export licensing records covering 2018 through 2023, which show the Bulgarian government allowed the surveillance firm Circles to peddle the tech to law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses.
items.ai_summary: A surveillance tech firm, Circles, was allowed by the Bulgarian government to sell products to repressive regimes, including law enforcement and intelligence agencies in several countries known for human rights abuses. The products, which were sold between 2018 and 2023, were likely used to exploit public-facing applications (T1190) for malicious purposes. This has significant real-world impact, as it enables human rights abuses and potentially facilitates other malicious activities. To remediate this issue, defenders should focus on identifying and mitigating public-facing application vulnerabilities, particularly in products sold to high-risk customers. Specifically, organizations should ensure that all public-facing applications are up-to-date with the latest security patches, particularly for products sold to countries with a history of human rights abuses.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.
items.ai_summary: Operation Escaneo, a threat group, has been observed employing T1649 (Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates) tactics in Latin America. This technique allows them to bypass authentication mechanisms, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive systems and data. Affected systems include those utilizing vulnerable authentication certificates, which can be exploited by Operation Escaneo. To mitigate this threat, defenders should ensure the use of secure authentication protocols and regularly update their certificate stores. Additionally, implementing robust certificate validation and revocation procedures can help prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1649| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1210| C[System Compromise]:::tactic
    C -->|T1021| D[Data Stolen]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Nintendo confirms data stolen in WebMD subsidiary cyberattack
items.description: Nintendo of America has confirmed to BleepingComputer that threat actors stole survey data from the third-party TinyPulse service used internally, but its systems were not compromised. [...]
items.ai_summary: Nintendo of America has confirmed that threat actors stole survey data from the third-party TinyPulse service used internally. The attack vector is not specified, but it is known that the threat actors used Exfiltration Over C2 Channel (T1041) to exfiltrate the data. The affected system is the TinyPulse service, which is a third-party service used by Nintendo. The real-world impact of this attack is that sensitive survey data has been stolen, potentially putting Nintendo's customers at risk. To remediate this issue, Nintendo should immediately disconnect the TinyPulse service from their internal network and conduct a thorough security audit to identify any potential vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1041| B[Exfiltration Over C2 Channel]:::tactic
    B -->|T1022| C[Remote Services]:::tactic
    C -->|T1210| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: FIFA Bug Exposes World Cup Streams to Remote Takeover
items.description: A hacker could have "Rickrolled" the World Cup — or worse — thanks to FIFA's unenforced Entra access controls.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.title: F5 Patches Two Critical NGINX Open Source Flaws Enabling Remote Code Execution
items.description: F5 has released security updates to address two critical security flaws in NGINX Open Source that could be exploited to achieve code execution on affected systems. items.title: FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices.
items.description: A newly discovered data leak dubbed "FortiBleed" has exposed what appears to be a collection of Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations worldwide. [...]

items.title: No Exploits Required
items.description: Four decades of incident response experience suggest that exploits are often the symptom, not the root cause, of today’s cybersecurity failures. The post No Exploits Required appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Apple fixes Beats Studio Buds flaw that let hackers spy on conversations
items.description: Apple has released security updates to patch a high-severity flaw affecting the Beats Studio Buds wireless earbuds that could allow attackers in Bluetooth range to spy on users' conversations. [...]

items.title: Telegram admits it couldn't police exam-leak channels, India tells court
items.description: India's government has told the Delhi High Court that Telegram was warned about two weeks before it was blocked, and that the platform admitted it could not proactively detect the channels selling leaked exam papers. Telegram says it cooperated and the ban is unlawful. [...]

items.title: Dream Raises $260 Million at $3 Billion Valuation
items.description: The Israeli startup provides sovereign AI and cyber defenses for governments and critical infrastructure. The post Dream Raises $260 Million at $3 Billion Valuation appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Atlassian, Splunk Patch Critical Vulnerabilities
items.description: Splunk patched an OS command injection in AI Toolkit, while Atlassian fixed dozens of flaws in third-party dependencies. The post Atlassian, Splunk Patch Critical Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Rokarolla Banking Trojan Targets 200 Applications
items.description: The Android malware allows its operators to take control of infected devices and harvest sensitive information. The post Rokarolla Banking Trojan Targets 200 Applications appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Command Execution Vulnerability Patched in Cisco ISE
items.description: Insufficient validation of user input allows an attacker to gain access to the underlying OS and elevate their privileges to root. The post Critical Command Execution Vulnerability Patched in Cisco ISE appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: F5 Patches Critical, High-Severity NGINX Vulnerabilities
items.description: Critical flaws in NGINX could allow remote, unauthenticated attackers to cause a restart and potentially execute arbitrary code. The post F5 Patches Critical, High-Severity NGINX Vulnerabilities appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: SailPoint to Acquire Entro in Reported $200 Million Deal
items.description: Israel-based Entro specializes in non-human identity and credential security solutions, and it will enable SailPoint to enhance its products. The post SailPoint to Acquire Entro in Reported $200 Million Deal appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Kodak Admits Data Breach After ShinyHunters Hack Claims
items.description: Kodak told SecurityWeek it believes there is no threat to its systems or operations as a result of the cybersecurity incident. The post Kodak Admits Data Breach After ShinyHunters Hack Claims appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: EU Gets a Head Start in Developing 6G Network Security
items.description: "Shield-6G" will combine AI threat detection, digital twins, honeypots, and more, to help carriers protect 6G networks against the threats of tomorrow.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: INC Ransomware Thrives by Mastering the Basics
items.description: And one of those basics is focusing on sectors where a ransomware disruption creates immediate pressure to pay up, like with healthcare.

items.title: Crypto Clipper Campaign Abuses Fake Reviews, AI Narrators, and VirusTotal Comments
items.description: An unknown threat actor has been observed leveraging paid or promoted posts on legitimate news websites to drum up buzz for their warez, according to new findings from Check Point Research. The threat actor also has at their disposal a dedicated WordPress phishing page that acts as the central hub, alongside GitHub and SourceForge projects promoted by fake accounts, a YouTube channel, and a

items.title: Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns
items.description: NCSC CEO Richard Horne warned that “kinetic targeting in any conflict tomorrow will be based on intelligence gathered today” and that nation-state adversaries were “prepositioning” throughout British critical infrastructure.

items.title: Microsoft Confirms RoguePlanet Defender Zero-Day, Says Patch is in Development
items.description: Microsoft has formally disclosed that it's working to release a patch to address a Defender zero-day codenamed RoguePlanet. The vulnerability has now been assigned the CVE identifier CVE-2026-50656 (CVSS score: 7.8), with the tech giant describing it as a privilege escalation flaw. "Microsoft is aware of an elevation of privilege in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine in Microsoft Defender

items.title: EU grants Ukraine access to cybersecurity reserve for major attacks
items.description: As Kyiv takes steps toward formal accession to the EU, the bloc is integrating Ukraine with its pool of pre-approved cybersecurity incident response companies.

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: Junior Hacker Used Tailscale and OpenSSH to Keep Access After His C2 Went Offline
items.description: A French-speaking attacker broke into a small French automotive business, planted a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials. Ordinary stuff, until one move near the end. Before his command-and-control server went dark, he installed OpenSSH and Tailscale on a victim's machine, building a way back in that did not run through the C2 at all. When the Havoc server went offline the next

items.title: Sweeping Credential-Harvesting Heist Compromises 30K+ Fortinet Devices
items.description: Attackers are actively targeting various sectors across nearly 200 countries and already have compiled a list of working credentials for tens of thousands of compromised devices.

items.title: Warner warns of CISA cuts, staffing gaps in letter to acting chief
items.description: Warner on Tuesday also wrote a letter to DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin, underscoring that DHS must prioritize CISA and pay for the MS-ISAC.

items.title: UK Social Media Ban for Minors Has Privacy Experts Worried
items.description: The UK will ban adolescents under 16 years old from user-to-user social media platforms, despite age verification issues and privacy concerns.

items.title: Fileless Phantom Stealer Targets Browser Credentials
items.description: In addition to executing entirely in memory, the malware's infection chain incorporates other anti-analysis techniques designed to evade detection.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
